questions.explanation: The intersection $A \cap B$ collects the elements that belong to both sets. Only $3$ and $4$ appear in $A$ and in $B$, so $A \cap B = \{3, 4\}$. The set $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ is the union, not the intersection.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.explanation: The union $A \cup B$ collects every element belonging to either set, listing each only once. Combining $\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $\{3,4,5,6\}$ and removing the repeats of $3$ and $4$ gives $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$. The set $\{3, 4\}$ is the intersection instead.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $B = \{3, 4, 5, 6\}$. What is the set difference $A \setminus B$?
questions.explanation: The difference $A \setminus B$ keeps the elements of $A$ that are not in $B$. From $A = \{1,2,3,4\}$ we remove $3$ and $4$ because they also lie in $B$, leaving $\{1, 2\}$. The set $\{5, 6\}$ would be $B \setminus A$, the reverse difference.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A set $A$ has $5$ elements. How many subsets does $A$ have, including the empty set and $A$ itself?
questions.explanation: Each element is either included in a subset or not, an independent two-way choice, so a set of $n$ elements has $2^n$ subsets. With $n = 5$ this gives $2^5 = 32$ subsets. The value $25$ is $5^2$ and $10$ is $2 \times 5$, both of which misuse the rule.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: If $A = \{a, b\}$ and $B = \{1, 2, 3\}$, how many ordered pairs are in the Cartesian product $A \times B$?
questions.explanation: The Cartesian product $A \times B$ consists of all ordered pairs whose first entry comes from $A$ and second from $B$, so its size is $|A| \cdot |B|$. Here $|A| = 2$ and $|B| = 3$, giving $2 \times 3 = 6$ pairs. Adding the sizes to get $5$ is the common error; the rule multiplies them.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let the universal set be $U = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and $A = \{2, 4, 6\}$. What is the complement $A'$?
questions.explanation: The complement $A'$ contains exactly the elements of the universal set that are not in $A$. Removing $\{2,4,6\}$ from $U = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ leaves $\{1, 3, 5\}$. The complement is always taken relative to the universal set, which is why $1$, $3$, and $5$ are the answer.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: For finite sets, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$. If $|A| = 12$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$?
questions.explanation: By the inclusion-exclusion principle, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| = 12 + 9 - 4 = 17$. Subtracting the intersection once corrects for the elements counted twice in $|A| + |B|$. Forgetting that subtraction gives the wrong total of $21$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive when:
options: $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$ imply $(a, c) \in R$
questions.explanation: Reflexivity requires every element to be related to itself, that is, $(a, a) \in R$ for all $a \in A$. The condition $(a, b) \in R \Rightarrow (b, a) \in R$ describes symmetry, and the chaining condition describes transitivity. So reflexivity is precisely the presence of all the diagonal pairs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is symmetric when, for all $a, b \in A$:
options: $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, a) \in R$ imply $a = b$
$(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$ imply $(a, c) \in R$
questions.explanation: Symmetry means that whenever $a$ is related to $b$, $b$ is also related to $a$, so $(a, b) \in R \Rightarrow (b, a) \in R$. The condition forcing $a = b$ from both pairs being present is antisymmetry, a different property. The diagonal condition is reflexivity and the chaining condition is transitivity.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is antisymmetric when, for all $a, b \in A$:
options: $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, a) \in R$ imply $a = b$
$R$ has no pairs of the form $(a, a)$
questions.explanation: Antisymmetry says that if both $(a, b)$ and $(b, a)$ belong to $R$, then $a$ and $b$ must be the same element. This allows distinct elements to be related in at most one direction, as with the order relation $\le$. The first option describes symmetry, which is the opposite kind of condition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is transitive when, for all $a, b, c \in A$:
options: $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$ imply $(a, c) \in R$
$(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, a) \in R$ imply $a = b$
questions.explanation: Transitivity allows a relation to be chained: if $a$ is related to $b$ and $b$ to $c$, then $a$ must be related to $c$. This is exactly $(a, b) \in R \land (b, c) \in R \Rightarrow (a, c) \in R$. The diagonal condition is reflexivity, the reversal condition is symmetry, and the last option is antisymmetry.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: An equivalence relation on a set $A$ is a relation that is:
questions.explanation: An equivalence relation must satisfy all three of reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity; together these properties let it partition $A$ into equivalence classes. The combination reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive defines a partial order instead. Equality and congruence modulo $n$ are familiar equivalence relations.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: On the set of integers, define $a \sim b$ to mean $a - b$ is even. Which property does this relation fail to have, if any?
options: It is an equivalence relation, failing none of them
questions.explanation: Reflexivity holds because $a - a = 0$ is even; symmetry holds because if $a - b$ is even then $b - a = -(a - b)$ is even; transitivity holds because the sum of two even numbers $(a - b) + (b - c) = a - c$ is even. All three properties hold, so the relation, congruence modulo $2$, is an equivalence relation. It partitions the integers into the even and odd classes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: How many distinct relations are there from a set $A$ with $2$ elements to a set $B$ with $3$ elements?
questions.explanation: A relation from $A$ to $B$ is any subset of $A \times B$, and $A \times B$ has $|A| \cdot |B| = 2 \times 3 = 6$ ordered pairs. The number of subsets of a $6$-element set is $2^6 = 64$, so there are $64$ relations. The value $6$ counts only the pairs themselves, not the subsets.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.explanation: All three diagonal pairs $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)$ are present, so $R$ is reflexive. The only off-diagonal pairs are $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$, which occur together, so $R$ is symmetric. Because $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ both appear with $1 \ne 2$, the relation is not antisymmetric.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: In the matrix of a relation $R$ on a finite set, the entry in row $i$ and column $j$ is $1$ when:
questions.explanation: The relation matrix records membership: the entry $m_{ij}$ is $1$ precisely when the ordered pair $(a_i, a_j)$ belongs to $R$, and $0$ otherwise. The row index gives the first coordinate and the column index the second. Setting entries by $i = j$ alone would only capture the diagonal, which is reflexivity, not the full relation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: In terms of its relation matrix, a relation $R$ on a finite set is symmetric exactly when the matrix:
options: has all $1$s on the main diagonal
has all $0$s off the main diagonal
has exactly one $1$ in each row
questions.explanation: Symmetry means $(a_i, a_j) \in R$ if and only if $(a_j, a_i) \in R$, so $m_{ij} = m_{ji}$ for all $i, j$. That condition is exactly the statement that the matrix equals its transpose. Having all $1$s on the diagonal is reflexivity, a different property.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A function $f : A \to B$ is injective (one-to-one) when:
options: every element of $B$ is an image of some element of $A$
every element of $A$ has at least one image
distinct elements of $A$ have distinct images in $B$
$A$ and $B$ have the same number of elements
questions.explanation: Injectivity means no two distinct inputs share an output: if $a_1 \ne a_2$ then $f(a_1) \ne f(a_2)$, equivalently $f(a_1) = f(a_2)$ forces $a_1 = a_2$. The condition that every element of $B$ is hit is surjectivity, not injectivity. Every element of $A$ having an image is just the definition of a function, which holds automatically.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A function $f : A \to B$ is surjective (onto) when:
options: no element of $A$ has two images
every element of $B$ is the image of at least one element of $A$
questions.explanation: Surjectivity means the image of $f$ is all of $B$: for every $b \in B$ there exists some $a \in A$ with $f(a) = b$. The condition that distinct inputs give distinct outputs is injectivity, and the requirement that no input has two images is part of being a function at all. So onto is precisely the codomain being fully covered.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A function that is both injective and surjective is called:
questions.explanation: A function that is one-to-one and onto at the same time is bijective, and only bijections have a two-sided inverse. An identity function is one particular bijection, but not every bijection is the identity, so identity is too narrow. Constant and periodic describe other behaviours unrelated to being both injective and surjective.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{a, b\}$. How many functions are there from $A$ to $B$?
questions.explanation: A function from $A$ to $B$ assigns to each of the $|A|$ inputs one of the $|B|$ outputs independently, so the count is $|B|^{|A|}$. With $|B| = 2$ and $|A| = 3$, this is $2^3 = 8$. The reversed expression $3^2 = 9$ counts functions from $B$ to $A$ instead.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $f(x) = 2x + 3$. Which statement is correct?
options: $f$ is injective but not surjective onto $\mathbb{R}$
questions.explanation: If $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$ then $2x_1 + 3 = 2x_2 + 3$, which forces $x_1 = x_2$, so $f$ is injective. For any real $y$, solving $y = 2x + 3$ gives $x = (y - 3)/2$, a real input mapping to $y$, so $f$ is surjective onto $\mathbb{R}$. Being both injective and surjective, $f$ is bijective.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 5, 8\}$ and $B = \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Reason out the sizes of the intersection and union.
questions.solution: The elements belonging to both $A$ and $B$ are the common members of $\{1,2,3,5,8\}$ and $\{2,3,4,5\}$; there are {{1}} of them, which is $|A \cap B|$. By inclusion-exclusion, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$, where the subtraction is needed because {{2}}. Substituting $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$ gives $|A \cup B| =$ {{3}}.

options: the shared elements would otherwise be counted twice
the union is always smaller than either set
the intersection is never part of the union
gaps.answer: the shared elements would otherwise be counted twice

questions.explanation: The elements in both sets are $2$, $3$, and $5$, so $|A \cap B| = 3$. Inclusion-exclusion subtracts the overlap because the sum $|A| + |B|$ counts each shared element twice; this gives $|A \cup B| = 5 + 4 - 3 = 6$, matching the listed union $\{1,2,3,4,5,8\}$. The distractors misread the principle: the union is never smaller than either set, $A$ and $B$ are not disjoint since they share three elements, and the shared elements are certainly part of the union.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $A$ have $4$ elements and $B$ have $3$ elements. Reason out two related counts.
questions.solution: The size of the Cartesian product $A \times B$ comes from the {{1}} rule, so $|A \times B| = |A| \cdot |B| =$ {{2}}. The number of subsets of $A$ is a different kind of count: because each of the $|A|$ elements is independently included or excluded, it equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The Cartesian product pairs each of the $|A|$ first coordinates with each of the $|B|$ second coordinates, a two-stage independent choice, so the product rule gives $|A| \cdot |B| = 4 \times 3 = 12$; the sum rule $4 + 3 = 7$ would count a single choice between disjoint groups. The number of subsets is $2^{|A|} = 2^4 = 16$, since each element is independently in or out. The distractor $2 \cdot |A|$ is linear, $|A|!$ counts orderings, and $|A|^{|B|} = 4^3$ counts functions from $B$ to $A$, none of which counts subsets.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Classify a relation and a function using the standard vocabulary.
questions.solution: A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive is called an {{1}} relation; the three properties together let it partition its set into classes. If instead a relation is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive, it is a {{2}}. A function in which distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs is called {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Reflexive, symmetric, and transitive together define an equivalence relation, which partitions the set into equivalence classes. Swapping symmetry for antisymmetry gives a partial order instead, the key structural contrast (equality is an equivalence relation, while $\le$ is a partial order). A function sending distinct inputs to distinct outputs is injective (one-to-one); surjective means covering the whole codomain, and bijective requires both at once.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $B = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$, and consider functions $f : A \to B$. Reason about how many there are and whether an injective one is possible.
questions.solution: Each of the $4$ elements of $A$ can map to any of the $5$ elements of $B$, and these choices are independent, so the number of functions from $A$ to $B$ is {{1}}, which equals {{2}}. An injective function needs distinct outputs for distinct inputs, so one can exist only when {{3}}; since $|A| = 4$ and $|B| = 5$, an injective function {{4}} exist.
questions.explanation: Each of the $4$ inputs independently picks one of $5$ outputs, so by the product rule there are $5^4 = 625$ functions; the swap $4^5 = 1024$ wrongly raises the domain size to the codomain size, while $5 \times 4$ and $5!$ count other things. An injective function needs at least as many outputs as inputs, that is $|A| \le |B|$; here $4 \le 5$, so an injective function does exist (for instance send $1, 2, 3, 4$ to four distinct elements of $B$). Had $|A|$ exceeded $|B|$, the pigeonhole principle would force two inputs to share an output.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)
